exemplar.title: An investigation of the effect of context on memory recall

detailedExemplarData.title: An investigation of the effect of context on memory recall

subject.title: Psychology
subject.description:
IB Psychology is a Group 3 subject in the International Baccalaureate Diploma Programme (IBDP). The course examines human behavior and mental processes through scientific inquiry, exploring biological, cognitive, and sociocultural approaches to understanding behavior. Students develop critical thinking skills while investigating contemporary psychological research, research methods, and ethical considerations in psychological practice.

The course is available at Standard Level (SL, 150 teaching hours) and Higher Level (HL, 240 hours), with first assessment in 2027.

Assessment consists of Paper 1 (integration of concepts, content, and contexts through short-answer and extended-response questions: SL 2 hours, HL 2 hours, SL 50%, HL 40%), Paper 2 (application of psychological knowledge to research contexts: SL 1 hour, HL 2 hours, SL 25%, HL 20%), Paper 3 (HL only: research methods and data analysis, 1 hour, 20%), and Internal Assessment (research proposal development: SL 25%, HL 20%).

strengths: Aim is clearly stated and its contemporary relevance is convincingly explained.
Schema theory is accurately described and explicitly linked to the investigation.
Both null and research hypotheses state and operationalize IV and DV with precision.
weaknesses: Minor conflation of âschemasâ and âscriptsâ reduces theoretical nuance.
Rationale for relevance is somewhat generic and could link to specific modern contexts.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: All three strands meet the 5â6 descriptor: the aim is stated and relevance explained; the theory is described and linked; hypotheses include fully operationalized variables. Despite minor theoretical imprecision, the student fulfills every requirement for the top band, justifying the full 6 marks.

strengths: Purposive opportunity sampling is described and its choice is well justified in terms of participant characteristics.
Independent-groups design is not only described but explained, including rationale against repeated measures.
Controlled variables and materials are identified and linked to methodological reliability.
weaknesses: Justification of randomization (Wheel of Names) could be validated in more depth.
Materials list includes extraneous notation that may confuse reliability justifications.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Each of the three strands satisfies the 3â4 band: design is explained, sampling technique and choice are explained, and controlled variables/materials are explained. The detailed sampling rationale pushes this to the top of the band, meriting the full 4 marks.

strengths: Appropriate descriptive (mean, SD) and inferential (Mann-Whitney U) statistics are correctly applied and reported.
Findings are interpreted in relation to the hypothesis, with clear linkage of p-value and direction of effect.
Graphs are correctly chosen and address the hypothesis, and dispersion (range, IQR) is thoughtfully discussed.
weaknesses: Bar chart omits error bars, reducing transparency of variability.
Justification for Mann-Whitney U on ordinal grounds is conceptually debatable.
Graph resolution and labelling could be improved for clarity.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The student fulfils the 5â6 band by applying and interpreting both descriptive and inferential statistics accurately and linking findings to the hypothesis. Minor presentation and conceptual imprecisions prevent the perfect 6, so a score of 5 is warranted.

strengths: Strengths and limitations are directly linked to validity and reliability and explained in context.
Modifications are explicitly tied to identified limitations and justified in terms of methodological improvement.
Findings discussion refers back to schema theory and connects with both Bartlett and Bransford & Johnson studies.
weaknesses: Discussion is concise and could explore alternative cognitive explanations.
Modification rationales, while clear, lack deeper elaboration on implementation details.
Evaluation lacks critical depth beyond the primary theoretical framework.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: All strands meet the 5â6 descriptor: findings are discussed with theoretical reference, strengths/limitations are explained and relevant, and modifications are fully justified. The evaluation is solid but not exceptional, so the top band is acknowledged with a 5 rather than 6.

strandSelections.reasoning: A boxplot and bar chart are provided, each comparing the two conditions and clearly linked to the hypothesis.  Axes are labelled and the graphs visually match the data, though the bar chart omits error bars and the images are slightly low-resolution.  These small presentation issues justify a 5 rather than 6.

The student states the statistical results, interprets them in relation to the hypothesis (rejecting null, accepting research), and links them to the direction predicted.  The discussion incorporates mean differences and p-value, demonstrating understanding.  Interpretation is accurate but concise; a 5 is therefore awarded.

Both null and research hypotheses are written, the IV (title before vs no title) and DV (number of correctly recalled idea units out of 12) are explicitly stated, and the DV is operationalised with a fixed checklist.  The IV manipulation is likewise operationalised.  Because all rubric requirements are fully met with no evident error, the top mark of 6 within the 5-6 band is justified.

Purposive opportunity sampling is named, the inclusion/exclusion criteria are detailed, demographics are given, and the rationale (shared academic background and English proficiency) is explained.  This satisfies the âexplainedâ expectation and is done with slightly more depth than the minimum, so a 4 (upper band) is awarded.

Schema theory is described with reference to Bartlett and the concept of scripts; the Bransford & Johnson study is summarised to illustrate the core mechanism.  The student then links this theory directly to the present investigation, explaining that providing a title should activate relevant schemas and improve recall.  Minor inaccuracies (e.g., equating âscriptsâ and âschemasâ without nuance) prevent a perfect score, but the description and link are both present and largely accurate, meriting the 5-6 band at a 5.

Findings are linked back to schema theory and both Bartlett (1932) and Bransford & Johnson (1972).  The student explains why activated schemas aid recall, showing engagement with theory.  However, the discussion is relatively brief and lacks critical depth or alternative explanations, positioning it at the lower end of the 5-6 band, hence a 5.

The student clearly states the aim â to test the effect of schema activation (title vs noâtitle) on recall of an ambiguous passage â and then justifies why this is still relevant today (technological change, 52-year gap, partial replication).  This moves beyond a mere identification by providing a concise explanation of contemporary value, although the justification is brief and somewhat generic.  Overall, the work satisfies the descriptor for band 5-6 but lacks the depth or multiple lines of argument expected for the very top of the band, so a 5 is appropriate.

Several relevant strengths (independent groups, standardisation, idea-unit checklist) and limitations (individual differences, narrow sample, live reading variability) are identified and each is explained.  The analysis directly links these points to validity or reliability, fulfilling band 5-6 criteria.  Depth is good but not exceptional, so a 5 is assigned.

Controlled variables (separate rooms, standardised instructions, single reader, timing) and materials are listed, and a brief justification is given for the key material (passage chosen to lack context).  Explanations are present but somewhat surface-level and uneven, warranting a 3 within the 3-4 band.

Two concrete modifications (matched-pairs design; using a recorded passage) are proposed, each explicitly tied to a stated limitation and justified in terms of how it would improve validity or reliability.  Rationale is clear but not extensively elaborated, warranting a solid 5 within the top band.

The student not only describes that an independent-groups design was used but also explains why repeated measures was rejected (practice/expectancy effects).  This constitutes an explanation rather than a description.  Some rationale (control of rehearsal bias) is sound but not especially detailed, placing the work solidly within the 3-4 band; a mid-band score of 3 is appropriate given the limited depth.

Descriptive statistics (mean, SD) and an appropriate inferential test (Mann-Whitney U for two independent samples with non-parametric assumptions) are applied.  The calculation appears accurate (U = 253.5, p = .028).  The justification that data are âordinalâ is debatable, but does not affect the correctness of the test selected.  Minor conceptual imprecision prevents full marks, so a 5 is assigned.

annotations.comment: The description of schema theory accurately captures Bartlettâs concept of prior knowledge guiding information processing. Consider explicitly distinguishing between schemas and scripts to enhance theoretical precision.
The summary of Bartlettâs (1932) study is thorough, but it conflates scripts and schemas without nuance. Suggest elaborating how scripts are a subset of schemas rather than equivalent.
The rationale for the investigation aim notes the 52-year gap and digital context but remains generic. Deepen the relevance by linking specific modern media effects on schema activation.
Both null and research hypotheses clearly state IV and DV and operationalise them with predetermined idea units and title manipulation. Excellent clarity here.
The independent-groups design is well explained with valid concerns about practice effects. To strengthen, include more detail on how randomisation via âWheel of Namesâ was validated.
Sampling description is detailed, but the justification for opportunistic sampling could be deepened by discussing potential selection biases and mitigation.
The materials list is exhaustive, but explaining why each piece (e.g., consent forms, stopwatch) was vital for reliability would deepen the methodological justification.
Controlled variables are comprehensively listed, but many materials include extraneous mathematical notation (e.g., \omega_eff). Remove or clarify these to avoid confusion.
Table 1 correctly presents mean and SD values, aiding clarity. For completeness, include sample sizes explicitly in the table caption.
The boxplot and bar chart are visually linked to the hypothesis, but the bar chart lacks error bars, reducing inferential transparency.
Interpretation stating overlap suggests no difference contradicts inferential results. Clarify that visual overlap may occur despite statistical significance.
The analysis of range and IQR demonstrates nuanced understanding of dispersion, linking variability to cognitive differences. Well done.
Inclusion of a Dixon Q test for outliers is unconventional for simple two-group data. Recommend focusing on IQR-based criteria or sensitivity analyses instead.
Justifying Mann-Whitney U on the basis of ordinal data is debatable. Discuss the scale level of idea-units and consider parametric alternatives if assumptions hold.
The calculation and reporting of U = 253.5 and p = .028 are accurate. However, referencing a website calculator in-text should be replaced with proper APA test citation.
Statement linking inferential results to hypothesis acceptance is clear and correctly interprets significance. Good alignment with research question.
Discussion references schema theory effectively. To deepen evaluation, consider alternative cognitive explanations (e.g., dual-process memory models).
The matched-pairs modification is well justified by limitations on individual differences. Consider discussing matching variables in more depth (e.g., memory aptitude).
Identifying narrow sample generalizability as a limitation is appropriate. To strengthen, propose specific age or cultural groups for future research.
References lack consistent APA formatting (e.g., missing italics for journal titles). Apply a standard citation style for professional presentation.
